cross_cell_repeated_theses.thesis: you scan the room for who seems calmer and measure your anxiety against their apparent ease.
you believe the only safety is being as unruffled as everyone else appears to be.
you've lost the ability to be surprised by ease because you're always checking who seems to have more of it.
you let the anxiety be present before you've matched anyone else's composure, because that was never the exit.
you release the need to be least afraid in the room and keep the attunement that taught you to read danger early.
you measure your limits against people who seem to give endlessly, and that comparison makes every no feel like failure.
you believe you're only good if your generosity outlasts everyone else's on the scoreboard.
you've lost the ability to receive without checking whether you've given as much as everyone else.
you set a boundary before you've out-given anyone, because winning the sacrifice contest was never the point.
you release the need to out-give everyone and keep the care that knows what actually deserves your energy.
you've been still and small to honor who you lost, and that stillness became your whole identity.
you believe your love is measured in how much of your aliveness you've been willing to forfeit.
you've lost the companion who understood your language before any words were spoken.
you live while grief is still fresh, before it becomes safe to live.
you release the paralysis and keep the love that made you still.
you scan every room for evidence that you belong, and the scoreboard in your chest never shows you winning long enough to rest.
you believe your worth is a placement on a ladder, and you can only rest once you're unquestionably at the top.
you've lost the ability to enjoy what's yours, because you only see it next to someone else's more.
you choose your own direction before you've won the comparison, because placement was never the point.
you release the need to win the comparison and keep the discernment that knows what is actually yours.
you benchmark your stamina against colleagues who never slow down, and that scoreboard keeps you running past empty.
you believe your value is proven only when your output exceeds everyone else's in the room.
you've lost the ability to rest without scanning whether you've earned it against everyone else's output.
you set something down before you've outperformed anyone, because winning the depletion contest was never the point.
you release the need to outlast everyone and keep the diligence that knows what actually deserves your energy.
you replay how others would decide because measuring your thinking against theirs feels safer than trusting your own.
you believe the only correct answer is whichever one the smartest person in the room would land on.
you've lost the quiet between thoughts, the only place where a decision yours could live without a rival.
you choose before the loop ranks every option against theirs, because consensus was never going to hand you certainty.
you release the need to think your way past everyone else and keep the discernment that lives outside the contest.
